short: A spelling change in the stem of a Class I adjective when forming the plural, to keep a sound or drop an unstressed -i.
long: Italian Class I adjectives ending in -co, -ca, -go, -ga sometimes insert an h in the plural to preserve the hard c/g sound (bianco -> bianchi, lunga -> lunghe). Adjectives ending in -cio, -gio, -cia, -gia drop the unstressed -i when forming the plural (vecchio -> vecchi, grigia -> grige). Whether the h appears can depend on stress (bianco -> bianchi but simpatico -> simpatici). A small set of words are irregular (amico -> amici despite stress, antico -> antichi despite stress).

short: Inserting an h in the plural of a Class I adjective ending in -co, -ca, -go, -ga to keep the hard c or g sound (bianco -> bianchi).
long: Italian doesn't have a separate letter for the hard-c-before-i sound, so an h is written in: bianco -> bianchi (not bianci, which would soften the c). Same for -ga -> -ghe (lunga -> lunghe). The rule is reliable when stress falls on the penult (bianco, lungo), but the antepenult-stress group goes the other way (simpatico -> simpatici with soft c). The most common kind of stem change.

short: Stress on the second-to-last syllable. Bianco (bian-co) has penultimate stress.
long: Most Italian words have penultimate stress (parola, finestra, bambino). The 'penult' is the second-to-last syllable. Stress position matters for the -co/-go pluralisation rule: penult-stressed adjectives take -chi/-ghi (bianco -> bianchi), antepenult-stressed take -ci/-gi (simpatico -> simpatici).

short: Stress on the third-to-last syllable. Simpatico (sim-pa-ti-co) has antepenultimate stress.
long: Less common than penultimate stress, but a stable pattern in adjectives like simpatico, fantastico, pratico, magico. The 'antepenult' is the third-to-last syllable. Antepenult-stressed adjectives in -co take the soft-c plural -ci, in contrast to penult-stressed adjectives which take -chi.

short: When the adjective comes after a copular verb (essere, sembrare): 'la casa e' rossa'.
long: The adjective sits on the other side of a linking verb from its subject. 'Il libro e' nuovo', 'sembra felice'. The adjective still agrees in gender and number with the subject. Contrasts with attributive position, where the adjective sits inside the noun phrase next to the noun.

short: When the adjective sits next to its noun in a noun phrase: 'la casa rossa'.
long: The adjective is part of the noun phrase, either before or after the noun. 'Una macchina nuova' (post-noun), 'un bravo studente' (pre-noun). Contrasts with predicative position, where the adjective sits across a linking verb from the subject.

short: The adjective sits before the noun: 'un bel libro', 'una piccola casa'.
long: A small set of Italian adjectives (bello, buono, grande, piccolo, nuovo, vecchio, giovane, bravo and a few others) canonically sit before the noun in their unmarked use. For most descriptive adjectives (colours, nationalities, categories), pre-noun position is marked or stylistically loaded.

short: The adjective sits after the noun: 'la casa rossa', 'un libro interessante'. The default for most descriptive adjectives.
long: Most Italian adjectives sit after the noun. Colours, nationalities, shapes, categories, and most evaluative adjectives default here. A small set of common adjectives (bello, buono, grande, etc.) prefer pre-noun position in their unmarked use.

short: The position an adjective takes when there's no special emphasis or shift in meaning. 'Un bel libro' is unmarked; 'un libro bello' is marked.
long: For most descriptive adjectives, the unmarked position is post-noun. For the small set including bello, buono, grande, piccolo, nuovo, vecchio, the unmarked position is pre-noun. Marked positions are still grammatical but carry an emphasis or meaning shift the unmarked version doesn't.

short: A change in meaning depending on whether the adjective sits before or after the noun. 'Un vecchio amico' (long-standing friend) vs 'un amico vecchio' (elderly friend).
long: A subset of pre/post-noun adjectives carry different readings in the two positions. Pre-noun is usually figurative or relational (un caro amico = beloved); post-noun is usually literal (un amico caro = expensive). Classic cases: vecchio, grande, povero, caro, nuovo, unico. A real source of meaning-misses for English-speaking learners.

short: A variant form of the same word, conditioned by the surrounding context. Bello has the allomorphs bel, bello, bell', bei, begli depending on what follows.
long: The pre-noun form of bello, quello, buono, and santo varies depending on what the following noun starts with, paralleling the form of the article. Bello -> bel libro (il), bello zaino (lo), bell'amico (l'), bei libri (i), begli zaini (gli). The technical name for these context-conditioned variants is 'allomorphs'.

short: When a plural noun phrase contains both genders, the adjective takes masculine plural form: 'i ragazzi e le ragazze sono italiani'.
long: Italian (like most Romance languages) defaults to masculine plural agreement when the antecedent is mixed-gender. This holds even when the feminines outnumber the masculines. A common English-influenced miss is proximity-agreement (agreeing with the closest noun), which gives the wrong italiane.

short: An adjective that doesn't change form for gender or number: rosa, blu, viola, marrone, beige, pari.
long: A small but important set of Italian adjectives never inflect. Colour terms borrowed from nouns (rosa = the flower, blu, viola, marrone, beige, fucsia, lilla, indaco) are invariable: 'le case rosa', 'i pantaloni blu'. Also pari, dispari, impari. Two-word compound colours (verde scuro, blu marino) are invariable as a unit.

short: A two-word colour expression that stays invariable as a unit: verde scuro, blu marino, giallo limone.
long: When a colour is qualified by a second word (light, dark, navy, lemon), the whole expression becomes invariable. 'Le pareti verde scuro' not 'verdi scure'. The same compound rule applies to most two-word colour expressions in Italian.
